summary: Gruber responds to Cory Doctorow's argument that curly quotes and typographic punctuation should be avoided on the web because they break RSS feeds and cause compatibility problems. As the author of SmartyPants, Gruber argues the solution isn't to retreat to ASCII but to demand smarter software that handles character encoding properly. He defends Robin Williams's typography advocacy and argues that 400 years of typesetting knowledge shouldn't be abandoned because of lazy software engineering. He provides a practical BBEdit AppleScript for converting smart punctuation to ASCII as a stopgap, while insisting the real fix is better tools like Dean Allen's Textile that handle entities automatically.
key_points: The problem with curly quotes on the web isn't the characters themselves but software that fails to handle HTML entities properly
ASCII is a cripplingly deficient character set and retreating to it is a step backward, not a pragmatic solution
Traditional typographic conventions from 400 years of print are a body of knowledge to learn from, not obsolete relics to discard
The web's distinction between HTML source and rendered output is precisely why HTML entities exist — to display proper typography from plain ASCII source
The responsibility lies with software developers to write tools that handle smart punctuation transparently, not with writers to dumb down their text
money_quotes: Doctorow's solution is for everyone to just stick with 7-bit ASCII characters. My solution is to fix or discard any retarded software that still insists on such restrictions.

And exactly whose paradise is ASCII? The ASCII character set is comprised of a measly 128 characters, many of which are non-printing control characters. It's the crummiest character set imaginable, the lowest of lowest common denominators.

The idea that web designers ought to ignore the lessons of traditional typesetting is as silly as recommending photographers ignore the lessons of composition, framing, and color from the art of painting.
That print typesetting is a 400-year-old art form doesn't make it obsolete. Quite the opposite, it means we're fortunate enough to have a tremendous body of knowledge to learn from.
Even French? How generous! Finally, the millions of people whose names contain characters such as 'é' can spell their names correctly without fear of offending Cory Doctorow's sensibilities.
Dealing with HTML entities is a trivial software problem. Trivial. You shouldn't have to deal with HTML entities by hand.
Proper typographic punctuation is 400-year-old news. That it's considered exotic, or even non-standard, on today's web is embarrassing.
The solution isn't for everyone to limit themselves to the same character set used on 1970's-era VT-100 terminals. The solution is for software developers to write smarter software.
key_insight: When software can't handle proper typography, the answer is to fix the software, not to abandon centuries of typographic craft for the lowest common denominator.
